quiz_questions.question_text: Multiple regression uses:

options.text: Only categorical variables
Two or more predictor variables
No predictors
One predictor

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiple regression includes multiple explanatory variables to predict a single response.

quiz_questions.question_text: In ŷ = b₀ + b₁x₁ + b₂x₂, b₁ represents:

options.text: The correlation
The intercept
The total effect of all variables
The change in ŷ per unit increase in x₁, holding x₂ constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Each coefficient in multiple regression controls for the other variables in the model.

quiz_questions.question_text: Adjusted R² is preferred over R² because it:

options.text: Penalizes for unnecessary predictors, preventing overfitting
Equals r
Is easier to compute
Is always higher

quiz_questions.explanation: Adjusted R² only increases if the new variable genuinely improves the model beyond what random chance would provide.

quiz_questions.question_text: ŷ = 5 + 2x₁ − 3x₂. When x₁ = 3 and x₂ = 1, ŷ = ?
Multicollinearity occurs when:

options.text: There is no correlation
y is correlated with x
The model has one predictor
Predictor variables are highly correlated with each other

quiz_questions.explanation: Correlated predictors make it hard to separate their individual effects on the response.

quiz_questions.question_text: Overfitting means the model:

options.text: Is too simple
Has R² that is too low
Fits noise in the training data and performs poorly on new data
Has too few predictors

quiz_questions.explanation: Too many predictors capture random noise rather than true patterns.

quiz_questions.question_text: The F-test in regression tests whether:

options.text: Multicollinearity exists
The intercept equals zero
The overall model is significant (at least one predictor is useful)
Individual predictors are significant

quiz_questions.explanation: The F-test evaluates the model as a whole, testing whether at least one predictor has a nonzero coefficient.

quiz_questions.question_text: R² always increases when adding more predictors because:

options.text: Better predictors are always added
Any new variable captures at least some noise, reducing residuals slightly
R² has no maximum
Mathematics requires it by definition

quiz_questions.explanation: Even random variables reduce the sum of squared residuals slightly, so R² can only stay the same or increase.

quiz_questions.question_text: Multiple regression can include categorical predictors by using:

options.text: A separate regression for each category
Dummy (indicator) variables coded as 0 or 1
Their raw category names
Only the mode of the category

quiz_questions.explanation: Categorical variables are encoded as indicator (dummy) variables so they can be included in the regression equation.

quiz_questions.question_text: VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) > 10 suggests:

options.text: The predictor is not significant
Problematic multicollinearity for that predictor
R² is too low
The model fits perfectly

quiz_questions.explanation: High VIF indicates that a predictor is highly correlated with other predictors, inflating the variance of its coefficient estimate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is multiple regression?

options.text: Running simple regression multiple times on the same data
A regression model with no intercept
A regression model that uses two or more explanatory variables to predict a single response variable
A method for predicting two response variables simultaneously

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiple regression extends simple regression by allowing more than one predictor to explain variation in a single response.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'holding other variables constant' mean when interpreting a coefficient?

options.text: The other variables are removed from the model
Only the intercept changes
All variables must have the same value
The coefficient shows the effect of one predictor on y while assuming the other predictors do not change

quiz_questions.explanation: Each coefficient isolates the effect of its predictor by controlling for all other variables in the model.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is multicollinearity?

options.text: When the response variable is correlated with itself
When the residuals are correlated with x
When R² is very low
When two or more predictor variables are highly correlated with each other, making it difficult to isolate their individual effects

quiz_questions.explanation: Multicollinearity means predictors share redundant information, making their individual coefficients unstable.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is adjusted R²?

options.text: The correlation between the two most important predictors
The same as R² but always higher
R² multiplied by the number of predictors
A modified version of R² that penalizes for adding predictors that do not meaningfully improve the model

quiz_questions.explanation: Adjusted R² accounts for the number of predictors, only increasing when a new variable provides a genuine improvement.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a dummy variable?

options.text: An insignificant predictor
A variable with missing data
A binary (0 or 1) variable created to represent a categorical predictor in a regression model
A variable that has been log-transformed

quiz_questions.explanation: Dummy variables code categories numerically (e.g., male = 0, female = 1) so they can enter a regression equation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does an interaction term (x₁ × x₂) allow in a regression model?

options.text: The effect of x₁ on y to depend on the level of x₂
Only main effects to be estimated
The model to become simpler
The intercept to be set to zero

quiz_questions.explanation: Interaction terms capture situations where the combined effect of two predictors is different from the sum of their individual effects.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the F-test assess in multiple regression?

options.text: Whether R² equals 1
Whether each individual coefficient is nonzero
Whether the model as a whole explains a significant amount of variation — that is, whether at least one predictor is useful
Whether the residuals are normally distributed

quiz_questions.explanation: The F-test compares the model with predictors to a model with no predictors (intercept only) to determine overall significance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'overfitting' mean in the context of regression?

options.text: The model captures random noise in the training data, causing it to perform poorly on new data
R² is too low
The model is too simple to capture the true relationship
All residuals are zero

quiz_questions.explanation: An overfit model memorizes the specific data it was trained on rather than learning the underlying pattern.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is stepwise regression?

options.text: Always including every available predictor
A method that adds or removes predictors one at a time based on statistical criteria to find a good subset of variables
Removing the response variable at each step
Running the regression in multiple steps, each with different data

quiz_questions.explanation: Stepwise methods automate variable selection, though they have limitations such as dependence on the order of variable entry.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) measure?

options.text: The amount of variation in y explained by the model
How much the variance of a regression coefficient is inflated due to multicollinearity with other predictors
The size of each residual
The overall significance of the model

quiz_questions.explanation: VIF quantifies how much a predictor's coefficient variance increases because of its correlation with other predictors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital uses multiple regression to predict length of stay (y, days) from patient age (x₁), severity score (x₂), and number of procedures (x₃): ŷ = 1.5 + 0.03x₁ + 0.8x₂ + 1.2x₃. For a 60-year-old patient with severity score 5 and 2 procedures, what is the predicted stay?

options.text: 7.7 days
6.5 days
9.7 days
10.3 days

quiz_questions.question_text: A real estate model predicts house price (y, in $1,000s) using square footage (x₁), number of bedrooms (x₂), and a dummy variable for pool (x₃ = 1 if pool, 0 if not): ŷ = 50 + 0.1x₁ + 10x₂ + 25x₃. How much more is a house with a pool predicted to sell for, all else equal?
quiz_questions.explanation: The coefficient on the pool dummy variable is 25, meaning a pool adds a predicted $25,000 to the house price, holding square footage and bedrooms constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marketing team builds a model with 15 predictors for customer spending. R² = 0.92 but adjusted R² = 0.71. What does this discrepancy suggest?

options.text: R² is always better than adjusted R², so use R²
The model needs even more predictors
Many of the 15 predictors are likely not contributing meaningfully; the model is probably overfitting
The model is excellent and all predictors are valuable

quiz_questions.explanation: A large gap between R² and adjusted R² indicates that many predictors inflate R² without genuine improvement, suggesting overfitting.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher predicting college GPA includes both SAT Math score and SAT Total score as predictors. The VIF for SAT Math is 18.5. What is the problem and what should she consider?

options.text: She should add more SAT subscores to the model
There is no problem; VIF of 18.5 is normal
SAT Math and SAT Total are highly collinear; she should consider removing one to reduce multicollinearity
She should ignore VIF because R² is what matters

quiz_questions.explanation: VIF > 10 signals severe multicollinearity. Since SAT Total includes SAT Math, these predictors are redundant and one should be removed.

quiz_questions.question_text: An insurance company models claim amount (y) using age (x₁), driving experience (x₂), and an interaction term (x₁ × x₂). The interaction coefficient is negative. What does this mean?

options.text: The effect of age on claim amount decreases as driving experience increases
The model is invalid
Age and driving experience are unrelated
Claim amounts are always negative

quiz_questions.explanation: A negative interaction term means the combined effect of age and experience reduces claims more than each factor alone would suggest; experience moderates the effect of age.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school district predicts student test scores using class size (x₁), teacher experience in years (x₂), and free lunch percentage (x₃). Adding x₃ raises R² from 0.58 to 0.59 but adjusted R² drops from 0.56 to 0.55. Should x₃ be kept?

options.text: Yes, because more variables always improve predictions
No — the drop in adjusted R² indicates that the marginal gain does not justify the added complexity; x₃ is likely not improving the model
No, because R² should never increase
Yes, because R² increased

quiz_questions.explanation: When adjusted R² decreases despite R² increasing, the new variable adds more noise than signal and the simpler model is preferred.

quiz_questions.question_text: A company models employee productivity (y) using hours of training (x₁) and years of experience (x₂). The coefficient for training is b₁ = 3.2 with p-value = 0.002, and the coefficient for experience is b₂ = 0.8 with p-value = 0.34. What should the company conclude?

options.text: Training is a statistically significant predictor of productivity, but years of experience is not significant in this model
Experience is more important because it has been measured longer
Both predictors are equally valuable
Neither predictor matters because one is insignificant

quiz_questions.explanation: A low p-value (0.002) for training indicates its coefficient is significantly different from zero; the high p-value (0.34) for experience means it does not add significant predictive value in the presence of training.

quiz_questions.question_text: A health study uses height (x₁, inches) and weight (x₂, lbs) to predict blood pressure. These predictors are correlated (r = 0.75). What is the practical consequence for the model?

options.text: Height and weight cancel each other out
Multicollinearity makes it difficult to determine the separate effect of height versus weight on blood pressure; coefficients may be unstable
The model cannot be run
The model will have R² = 0

quiz_questions.explanation: When predictors are correlated, their shared information makes individual coefficients harder to estimate precisely.

quiz_questions.question_text: A retailer adds 'day of the week' as a predictor in a sales model. Day has 7 categories. How many dummy variables are needed?
quiz_questions.explanation: For a categorical variable with k categories, k − 1 dummy variables are needed; one category serves as the reference level.

quiz_questions.question_text: An agricultural researcher models crop yield using rainfall (x₁), fertilizer (x₂), and sunlight hours (x₃). The overall F-test has p-value < 0.001, but the individual p-values for x₁ and x₃ are both above 0.05. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The model as a whole is significant, but individual coefficients may appear insignificant due to multicollinearity or shared information among predictors
Only fertilizer should be included
The model is useless because two of three predictors are insignificant
The F-test is wrong

quiz_questions.explanation: A significant F-test with individually insignificant predictors often points to multicollinearity — the predictors together explain variation, but their shared information prevents any single one from standing out.
